text: On page 2 at the top, the cost of the energy from TNR should read $200.CO per megawatt hour instead of $2.00.
Assuming a cost of $2.00 per pound for tubealloy and assuming that all the 25 is used, the cost of energy (not mechanical power) would be about $0.02 per megawatt hour.

Jeffries did obtain the following advance information based on the preliminary results, - a large number of granite bodies were found to contain up to 100 ppm of tubealloy and a small number up to 1,000 ppm.

For a reasonable utilization, the potential difference between the tube and wire would have to be of the order of magnitude of 5,000,000 volts and one could obtain a current of about 50 amperes if the system were run at 500,000 kw.
However, the thermo-electric constants of tubealloy are not known and one can estimate that the amount of power obtainable from a pile like that at W would only be of the order of 5,000 kw.
Watson suggested that it would probably not exceed 1/2 although Mr.

Wigner pointed out that by high temperature operation $(700^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ it is possible to obtain efficiencies up to around $65\%$ and so, as long as we would just bs making kilowatt hours, we should abandon all "crazy schemes" and think seriously about high temperature operation. "Goldbergs" are not attractive for power production and Mr.
Wigner will lead the investigation into the possibility of power producing piles utilizing the energy directly by endothermic chemical reactions [Item IBlb (3)].
Vernon will do the same for gas cooling [Item IBlb (2)].
